turns.content: I'm trying to port a legacy Python project into Rust, and I keep thinking my new code will be automatically clean like in the old days. It feels just like running lint tools on the previous repo but with more noise because we're using serde_json::Value everywhere instead of typed structs.

That is a common feeling when transitioning from interpreted languages to Rust; the verbosity comes from type safety rather than bugs. However, since you are building tooling yourself, you can suppress that noise by writing your own clippy lints with dylint so that only relevant issues trigger warnings instead of every single serde deserialization warning.

I've been looking at the documentation and it seems like I need to implement a trait for every single function in my workspace. That sounds exactly like what we did before but now we have to manually check if a function is safe or not, which feels redundant when using std::mem::replace instead of manual swapping.

You are correct that you cannot just copy the old pattern blindly; dylint requires implementing specific traits for your custom lints rather than checking every single function. Instead of manually swapping values everywhere, you should use the specialized macros provided by the crate to handle common patterns like field initialization or unused imports so your own lint logic stays focused on actual violations.

Okay, but I'm still confused about how to actually write a custom lint that checks if someone is using serde_json::Value when they could be using a typed struct. It feels like I need to parse every single line of code which seems inefficient compared to what we did before with simple regex patterns.

You do not need to parse every line manually; the framework generates the AST nodes for you, so you only inspect relevant parts of the expression tree instead of scanning raw text. You can define a custom lint that targets specific functions like serde_json::from_str and checks if the return type is actually a generic map rather than a concrete struct, which is much more efficient than regex matching.

So I should just create a new module with a trait implementation for my workspace lints? It feels like we have to write boilerplate code everywhere when using clippy instead of just adding flags to an existing config file.

Exactly, you must define your own custom lint within a dedicated module because the default configuration does not know about your specific rules. You will implement the required trait for your workspace lints and register it in Cargo.toml so that clippy recognizes your new rule rather than relying on generic flags.

This is getting complicated, I thought we could just add a flag to my existing config file like before? It seems like writing custom lints with dylint requires too much setup compared to what we did in the old project.

The old approach relied on pre-built rules that you simply enabled via flags without any code changes, but now you must write the actual logic yourself because those generic rules cannot detect your specific serialization patterns. You should create a new module with a trait implementation for your workspace lints so that clippy recognizes your new rule rather than relying on generic flags.
